title: Is My Child An Introvert?
subtitle: Twelve gentle questions to clarify temperament. Not a diagnosis. A starting point.
questions: After a busy day, does your child need quiet alone time before they can talk about it?
At parties, does your child watch first, then join (or not join at all)?
Does your child have one or two close friends rather than a wide group?
Does your child do their best thinking alone or in small groups?
Does your child get overwhelmed by loud, fast environments?
Does your child speak less in big groups but a lot one-on-one?
Does your child need warning before transitions?
Does your child pick up on the moods of people around them quickly?
Does your child prefer deep conversations to small talk, even with adults?
Does your child have a rich inner world (imaginary games, daydreams, complex pretend play)?
Does your child get cranky after long social days even when the day went well?
Does your child need more sleep than peers their age?

title: Is My Child Highly Sensitive?
subtitle: Twelve questions adapted from the temperament research literature.
questions: Does your child startle easily?
Does your child notice subtle changes in your mood?
Does your child complain about clothing tags, sock seams, or scratchy fabric?
Does your child get overwhelmed in noisy environments (gyms, cafeterias, malls)?
Does your child notice small details others miss?
Does your child react strongly to bright lights or strong smells?
Does your child cry easily over things others would brush off?
Does your child take a long time to warm up to new people?
Does your child ask deep questions for their age?
Does your child get rattled by quick changes in plan?
Does your child feel things very intensely (joy and sadness both)?
Does your child need extra recovery time after exciting events?

title: School Anxiety Screen
subtitle: A gentle screen for school-related anxiety. If most are 'often,' talk to a child clinician.
questions: Does your child complain of stomach aches or headaches on school mornings?
Does your child resist getting out of bed for school but not on weekends?
Does your child cry, freeze, or panic at drop-off?
Does your child ask repetitive questions about what will happen at school?
Has your child started asking to stay home in the past month?
Does your child report feeling sick at school but the nurse can't find anything?
Has your child's appetite changed on school days?
Does your child have trouble falling asleep on Sunday nights?
Does your child mention specific worries (a teacher, lunch, recess, a kid)?
Does your child seem fine at home and 'shut down' only at school?

title: After-School Restoration Needs
subtitle: How big is your child's recovery tank, really? Helps shape an after-school routine.
questions: How long does your child usually need before they can talk after school? (Less than 15 min, 15 to 30, more than 30, all evening)
What do they reach for first when they get home? (Snack, screen, bed, pet, alone time, you)
How does your child react to questions right after pickup? (Answers freely, snaps, goes silent, melts down)
When does the meltdown usually hit? (In the car, doorway, at dinner, at bedtime, none)
What helps fastest? (Quiet alone time, a snack, going outside, snuggling, screens)
How do weekends compare to school days? (Much calmer, somewhat calmer, the same, harder)
How is bedtime on school nights? (Easy, slow, a battle, very hard)
How does Friday afternoon compare to Monday afternoon? (Same, worse, better)
How long does the recovery take after a school event (field trip, play, party)? (Hours, a day, several days)
How is your child's appetite during the school week? (Normal, picky, low, high)

title: IEP or 504: Which Are We Heading Toward?
subtitle: A practical screen. Not a substitute for an evaluation. Use to gather your thoughts before you call the school.
questions: Has your child been diagnosed with anxiety, ADHD, autism, learning disability, or sensory processing disorder?
Is your child's anxiety affecting their access to instruction (missing class, refusing to attend, cannot perform on tests)?
Has the school already tried general accommodations and they didn't work?
Does your child need specially designed instruction (not just accommodations), for example explicit instruction in a learning skill?
Has the teacher mentioned concerns more than once?
Is the gap between potential and performance widening?
Have you put concerns in writing to the school yet?
Do you know the difference between accommodations and modifications?
Do you know your state's IEP timeline (referral to evaluation to meeting)?
Are you ready for a meeting where the team disagrees with you?

title: Sensory Profile Quick Screen
subtitle: Light, medium, or heavy sensory needs? A starting point, not a diagnostic tool.
questions: Does your child cover their ears in normal-noise environments?
Does your child crash into furniture, jump from heights, or seek heavy hugs?
Does your child gag at certain food textures?
Does your child refuse certain clothing on principle (jeans, socks, tags)?
Does your child avoid messy play (paint, sand, glue)?
Does your child seek messy play more than peers (always covered in something)?
Does your child react strongly to bright lights or fluorescents?
Does your child have trouble sitting still during meals or homework?
Does your child melt down in busy stores or events?
Does your child sleep better with weight on top of them?

title: Parent Self-Care Check
subtitle: You cannot pour from an empty cup. Ten honest questions for the parent of a sensitive child.
questions: When did you last sleep seven straight hours?
When did you last leave the house alone, with no errand attached?
Do you have one person you can text honestly about your child without being judged?
Have you eaten three real meals today?
Are you carrying your child's anxiety in your body (jaw, shoulders, stomach)?
When did you last move your body in a way that felt good?
Are you saying yes to school events you don't have the bandwidth for?
Have you had a check-up in the last year?
Do you have a therapist, coach, mentor, or oracle reader you can lean on?
Are you the only adult holding the emotional load of your child's school life?

title: Social Battery Baseline
subtitle: How much social fuel does your kid actually have? Helps with playdates, parties, and weekend planning.
questions: After a one-hour playdate, how is your child? (Energized, neutral, tired, melting down)
After a birthday party, how is your child? (Same options)
After a full school day, how is your child by 5 p.m.?
Can your child do back-to-back social events in one day? (Yes, sometimes, no, never)
How does your child respond to a surprise visitor? (Happy, neutral, withdraws, melts down)
How long can your child play in a group of three or more before needing a break? (Minutes, an hour, a few hours, all day)
Does your child prefer parallel play (next to a friend) or interactive play (with a friend)?
How does your child handle 'just one more' requests at the end of a play date?
How does your child describe the day after a sleepover?
How long does it take your child to recover from a school field trip?

title: Gentle Bedtime Fit
subtitle: Is your bedtime routine actually serving a sensitive nervous system? Ten honest questions.
questions: Does the bedtime routine start at the same time most nights?
Are screens off at least 60 minutes before sleep?
Is the bedroom cool, dark, and quiet?
Does your child have a wind-down activity that doesn't involve a screen?
Is your child eating dinner more than two hours before sleep?
Does your child have a way to discharge worries (worry jar, talk time, journal) before lights-out?
Is there a transitional object or comfort item available?
Are you available for a brief check-in after lights-out?
Is the wake time consistent on weekends as well as weekdays?
Does your child wake feeling rested most days?
